sentences.text: My name is Irving Gromis, I-R-V-N-G G-R-O-M-I-S.
I was born Horochow-- Horochow.
And-- H-O-R-O-C-H-O-W.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
And the street?
If you want to, sure.
I don't remember.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- October-- no.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- August 5, 1916.
I'm past 80.
Past 80.
Seven grandchildren, two sons.
Yeah.
Efraim, the father.
Midel is the mother.
Efraim.
E-F-R-I-M.
A-I-M, I guess, in English.
In Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH].
And the name Midel-- name Midel, M-I-D-E-L.
Maiden name is Kovler.
K-O-V-L-E-R.
Kovler.
Was same to mine.
Tevve.
Tevve is like the name Tevve.
OK?
He has another name.
It's Isaac-- [NON-ENGLISH].
But I didn't ever use it.
Yeah.
The brother-- the binding from the brothers-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] would say, is the basic-- is not the brother we born together, but we was-- when we was hiding, he was not have the shoes.
And I took--
Oh, I see.
That was the--
--brother, that's-- I was binded together with him.
Everything.
We share together.
Everything very close.
With the--
Yeah, but-- we sleep the same room.
But sometimes, we slept together and my mother slept extra-- next door.
Yeah.
My father's name is Efraim.
Didn't I say?
He did everything.
He-- when he was young, he was in the welding business.
He fix-- he makes that-- you know, you call it-- to make-- he welded, sometimes.
The-- and he was putting on tin in the roofs, in the churches, basically.
He was working.
But he got older, when he got married-- he's-- I don't know.
He went to America.
With his parents-- he has a-- a father over here.
He had two sisters over here and a brother.
And when I came over here, I meet him over there-- over here.
Uncle.
My aunt.
The father's older sister, which she took care on him.
He was-- he-- she came over here as a little girl.
But over there, she took care on him because he was the baby.
And she-- she keeps him as a mother.
They lived together.
Basically, they share views together.
And they are the differences between them.
They dealing between the difference people with-- they know each other.
They was cousins.
Father and mother was cousins.
And they share differences but they were very peacefully and settle down.
The difference between them.
And he was religious.
Over-religious, I would say-- could say.
He went to the rabbi always.
He-- he was with the rabbi.
And he came home-- Lwow.
And then he moved over-- I showed the picture.
He moved over with the rabbi in that little town, over there, which it says there.
And he-- there, he came three times in the year home.
And they were together with the-- my mother.
And she say, stay another week.
He say, I cannot stay.
It's not good for me.
It's not good for you.
Basically, they share-- they share together differences in opinions and everything nicely, peacefully.
Says, I cannot stay over here because she need-- you need help.
And I cannot help you.
My mother has a grocery store, wholesale.
She lost money on it.
She needs help.
He couldn't help her because she wasn't religious.
What I do remember, she has to carry packages home.
I went from school home.
Then I have to carry the packages to this lady, this lady, the night I'd carry the packages.
She says, I cannot ask you to do it.
[INAUDIBLE], he has a [INAUDIBLE], you know, it's-- then I have to do by myself.
And he say, I'll better go back to my job.
So he went back.
He came three times in the year, two times in the year.
But the difference in views they have together with each other, it was wonderful.
I learn a lot from them.
Your difference and I-- that we cannot change both of us.
Whether we are man and wife, we are cousins, we are tied up together.
And we-- only thing, we cannot stay together always.
Stay two or three weeks.
I have to go away.
And never fight.
And that's all the end to it.
He went back to Lwow.
Then they moved out already from Lwow to that city which I says there.
And I didn't see him anymore.
But then when the ghetto comes, I couldn't go, never.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's--
With the mother together.
Yeah.
Yeah.
The what?
The town was-- we lived in was about 5,000 families.
Horochow. 5,000 families.
Most Jews.
You know, Europe was most Jewish. 90-- 90% Jewish.
But they comes to a-- a-- a-- a gentile didn't sell the house, then they give him so much money for the house.
You could buy a village for this house.
Yeah.
Which, it happened.
They moved out, moved out, outside the city.
And the city became only a church-- became a Polish church.
That's all.
And around the church were Jews.
Because I was living-- going to the church.
But then I-- but the Russians.
And the--
I was, the beginning, I was-- in the beginning, like anybody, in Talmud.
I have a-- a picture.
I have a certificate.
My father was here.
My mother send him.
Then he says, going to shuls I don't like him to go.
I-- I'll show you the-- the school.
If you want to take a picture.
When the marks where the teacher make it, made me.
And [NON-ENGLISH].
Says, everything is going.
[NON-ENGLISH].
He likes yeshiva.
Then they send me to yeshiva [NON-ENGLISH].
And then I-- and eventually the Yeshiva with the Russian were nice, they says, but it's not.
I know a little bit of Russian, but not a big scholar.
They send me away to [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was the Russian yeshiva.
They call it, in Hebrew, [NON-ENGLISH], which is written in the books, still books today.
The one from the rabbis write in-- in-- in the-- the [INAUDIBLE].
And that Yeshiva was there, well, a couple months.
I have to bear payos.
I have to bear tzitzits.
You know the way it is.
It's not-- not necessary to wear it.
And I-- you have to davening exactly with the-- [NON-ENGLISH].
You know the way it is.
But I accept it.
But when I came home for a visit Passover, as my mother was going to her cousins, which they're very intelligent.
The name-- I'll tell you the name.
[NON-ENGLISH] Schechter.
[NON-ENGLISH] Flom, I had at that time.
And I came to there.
He says, listen, a rabbi, he wouldn't be.
He doesn't like it.
And he wouldn't be.
To-- to give him education like in the Jewish school, learn archaeology, biology, with all of that stuff with Darwin's theory about evolution, he accept it very nice.
He likes it, but what's at the end of it?
He wouldn't do anything.
The only way, he have to learn a trade.
What kind trade?
Tailor, this, that, I don't like.
I went to a tannery, tannery, tanning the leather.
Then, Offenbach, in Germany, it was the biggest-- the biggest tannery in the world, they say.
The biggest chemistry they have there.
It cost, at that time, I paid 120 dollars, a passport, a this, a that.
I went a couple weeks in 1932.
And I went a couple weeks Offenbach, close to Germany-- to Frankfurt.
I was there a couple weeks.
And then Hitler came to the power.
It was 1933, I guess.
I don't know exactly.
I came home.
Oh, with the mother.
The-- no.
We were-- the-- mom was a mother and she did the [INAUDIBLE].
Not modern lady, beautiful dress that you see in the picture, nice dressed.
Plus, she came, one from the telling people when they finish university in the Russia and they-- there.
And they told me the same.
He wouldn't be a rabbi.
He wouldn't be-- if he goes to be educated, he wants to be a doctor.
He would-- a doctor, he couldn't get anyway, the-- the-- he couldn't get the diploma.
Because Jewish people cannot be doctors.
The only thing-- learned a trade.
I went to Germany.
I learned a trade.
I came home.
He says, yes, but the-- the government wouldn't give you permission.
You have no-- you have no practice.
And they called it a [NON-ENGLISH].
Permission to work for yourself.
Then, anyway, I wrote to the president from Poland-- not the president.
He was the head from the-- Pilsudski.
I wrote him a letter, which I remember the letter, which I did wrote to him.
He send me, in two weeks, a permission to work leather if I could.
I had the permission, [NON-ENGLISH].
They called it [NON-ENGLISH].
In Polish, it's the [NON-ENGLISH].
And I had permission.
And then I started to work leather for myself.
It was also illegal for the-- for the-- to tax us, it was illegal.
I didn't pay taxes.
But I work--
My childhood was-- you know, what-- a childhood of-- in school, I did-- I liked the school.
I learned at school.
Which, I have the certificate from school.
And then, when I come 16, 15--
Basically, I-- I used to play violin.
I had two years of violin.
And I don't know the violin.
I played chess in the club.
It was a-- a club from chess.
You have to pay there.
I paid 2 zlotys a year whenever-- this-- whenever you come in.
Play chess with your partner.
You take a-- a tray with the grapes.
It was at that time, it was-- it pays money.
You have to pay for it.
And I make money on the side.
You asked me a question, how did I make money?
After my mother was now in the-- the business, and I carry up the packages, this lady, this lady-- after I took-- saccharin was illegal.
Saccharin.
Saccharin.
Was illegal in Poland because you have to buy sugar.
And I went to the-- this guy.
They knew me a lot.
Yeah, send the saccharin, I make a few dollars.
Once, it happened I find American $5.
I bring everything to the mother.
And so on, then later on, my uncle stepped in in the business.
They called it [NON-ENGLISH].
And I was standing to collect the dollar from everyone.
And I make a little money on the side.
Then, I went out, a name, [NON-ENGLISH], the biggest people, they need money.
They need $300.
And I went with them into partnership.
And we made money.
Couple weeks.
And they says, listen to me.
You are a young kid.
You don't have to go with us.
It's no good.
We have your money.
We-- money-- we-- they divided.
They throw me out from the business.
I went to the market again.
I bought myself a cow.
I haven't got in city where to keep it.
I give to a guy.
He kept a week.
Then my brother went and get milk every week.
You know, I had-- always, a cow, two cows.
I bought them myself.
It was $50, so a lot of money that time. $40 a cow.
Now, in one time, I lost $5.
My mother told me-- I'll never forget-- you're a businessman.
If you're losing-- making money, you're not a businessman.
But if you could lose $5, $6, then you're in the business.
Don't worry about it.
You'll get next week.
I remember every story my mother used to tell me.
Sadder stories.
Which I'd remember.
She was very smart.
And I used to come in-- they call it the [NON-ENGLISH], advisors, [NON-ENGLISH] in Hebrew.
They-- and a lady come, she have a [NON-ENGLISH].
Doesn't cost her any money.
I know Midel is smart.
And she says, I have two boys for my girlfriends.
One is this way.
One is-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH], a primitive man.
It's the [NON-ENGLISH].
Basically it's [NON-ENGLISH], a-- a-- a truck driver, you know, in a-- all driver.
This guy, basically, she says, if you get married, you have to expect to get a divorce.
The negative in marriage.
You have to get divorced.
Tell them, you get divorced, he says, son of a bitch, I don't need you.
You don't know-- the other guy says, listen.
The rabbi calls him.
He assess them.
You have to divorce nicely.
She's a human being.
[NON-ENGLISH], he says.
You have to divorce.
And then you have to-- to-- to go to rabbi.
And tell him you haven't got anywhere to go.
He's going to do it by himself.
I've been with her to the movies with my mother.
She took me when I was young. was the first time.
In Europe, the original theater.
I see that with my mother.
And at that time, we paid 2 zlotys, a lot of money.
She paid 2 zlotys, and a [INAUDIBLE] for me.
And we paid, and we see When I came in this country, I see Zero Mostel.
It was 1948, I think, the first time it playing over here.
I told them, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You know, that-- He says, listen.
This is-- you are on the other side.
I know you speaking not English well.
You know, he says, listen, why could I speak-- he says, the [INAUDIBLE] picture is different story.
As Zero Mostel So then, it was 1948, I guess.
A lot of--
To childhood.
What I want to say-- I went to school.
We each get the marks from there-- not high marks-- from the-- get for management, upperr management.
Because I was a shegetz.
You know, because years ago, they used to all be in the back, the-- the [INAUDIBLE] thing.
I used to [INAUDIBLE] And other things like this, other things I did.
1937, I went back in the-- in the army, yeah.
Yeah, but the army-- I-- I-- You want to talk about it?
I went to the army.
It was--
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] the second [NON-ENGLISH].
They send me down there.
And I have took the map.
Everybody could take the map.
But the Polacks, they didn't know that much of mapping.
They did [INAUDIBLE].
There was the top there.
And suddenly, they give me the map and I says, take these 10 people to this block, the other block.
And I knew the blocks already.
The first day, I knew [INAUDIBLE] the blocks, about new-- a new development, that time. 1937.
And I was there managing-- I had food, they had eat, and drink.
And suddenly, 1937, then I got it off-- your-- they're playing the chess.
The captain plays in chess some of the other.
And I ask him permission.
I have to say it-- Polish, shall I say Polish?
If you want.
[POLISH] Says, why, sure.
His national Polish.
[POLISH] And I start to look in the game.
He says, you know how to play?
Says, a little bit.
And he start to play his own game, the second game and that's how-- Then he says to me, you going to get first bent, in the-- first line, which I had it.
I lost it.
I was in the first line.
And the Polacks in Ukraine [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I take the first place in the first part.
And he send me to the barber, to make my hair cut.
He make me-- the barber make me the front like a little bit of hair.
And he says to me, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
[BACKROUND NOISE] He says, don't worry about the money.
Just give me the change.
Whenever I left, I give him $20 and so on.
I give him more and more.
I don't remember exactly.
But later, on, he gave me [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Nothing is for me to be in the army anymore.
A white ticket.
They call it a white ticket.
And I'll be using that ticket.
I went home.
Either way, they send me home.
I were a couple weeks, a couple-- about two months, something-- three, or-- and they send me home.
And the Captain Neiman, Dr.
Neiman was the-- he-- gave me the x-ray.
He told me to eat that stuff before.
You know, I've been told already what to do it.
And they find out I have a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That means a ulcer in the stomach.
Then, my mother--
A little bit.
The feel the everything.
But it's not comfortable.
You know, that we feel, sure.
They recognize right away.
I-- then, I went to my mother, did not quiet, he says, listen.
Something is there.
You not feel comfortable, you this-- you go to Lemberg-- Lwow.
The biggest doctor.
Which at that-- I meet a doctor over here, which was that-- the student.
Came to Lemberg.
Dr.-- a Ukrainish doctor.
Ukrainish doctor.
I've-- I spoke to him yesterday.
Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] Cherenesky in Lwow.
The biggest doctors.
And they are x-ray me.
And he says, you've got a couple figures down the stomach.
They are-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And my mother start to give me food.
They said, the only thing give, food.
And I went to the other doctor, and I meet this doctor over here, over here, which I speak to him every day, play with him in chess.
He was a student.
My doctor, my friend, went to-- Dr.
Erlich, which his name is [NON-ENGLISH] Erlich, from Horochow.
He says to her, I'll give you $10 to give my son down there.
And I come there.
It's very-- I had to walk up to university.
And he says-- and he says [NON-ENGLISH].
And I meet this Dr.
Erlich.
I meet him over here.
Which I could speak to him if you want me to.
I'll speak to the telephone.
And I spoke to him.
He doesn't remember exactly, and I don't remember.
But the other doctor gave me the $10.
And he show me all the doctor hospital with them.
And I went home.
And I feel OK.
I've nothing-- healthy.
And I said to mommy, feel-- feel not-- because I'm not healthy.
A young guy, 20-some years.
And that's it.
This was the doctor.
Yeah.
When I was in-- in-- 19-- '32, I exactly remember the date.
They-- and I-- I came home.

Then my mother and the cousins, the mother Schechter, advised me, if you finish yeshiva and it's cost you a lot of money, you finish, you couldn't get work because you haven't got the privilege, you haven't got practice, you have to get [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a working card from the government.
Then I wrote to Pilsudski, and as I said before, he send me a card.
OK?
And he send me a card.
And I have the card.
I kept the card all the time with me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] That was this place, [NON-ENGLISH].
They call it [NON-ENGLISH].
In Polish, it's [NON-ENGLISH].
This is the head from the card, which I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Know what else you would know?
Well, we're talking about Hitler's rise to power?
No This was in 1932--
I went back to Offenbach.
Offenbach is the main factory there.
In the 1933, we start to feel there an uncomfortable.
I went back down there-- and I mean, I didn't-- couldn't-- couldn't stay there.
Jews run away to America here.
Try to run away from all over.
Why should I stay there?
Then I didn't continue-- I didn't know anything.
About school, I know very little about chemistry, about the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I know a little bit.
But, when Hitler come, later on, I start to work in the primitive style, which I was a good [NON-ENGLISH], they call it.
Skinning it-- splitting the-- splitting-- the machine doesn't go it.
I split by hands and make sharper knife till today.
I sharp a knife, nobody else.
I work with the butcher line.
I can sharp the knife like any-- when I take the knife just out to tray, knives get sharp.
And you could shave it with it.
Then I got that same thing, to sharp the knife.
And, a lot of stories about.
And I make the leather by the Nazis.
Then I told you, I hided my leather because not registered.
And I hide it under the floor.
I knock off the floor.
Oh, let me see how it come.
That have come to the point--
I registered.
And I hided a lot of leather.
Somebody-- everybody make business.
You had some stuff.
The money was not worth it.
But you have leather.
And I got potatoes, or things like this.
We changed for each other according-- the-- before the war.
That's were the change.
Now, let me give you two examples.
How did it change, it was Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur.
I remember one guy, he has books, is fully religious.
And the other guy is-- only has horses-- skin from horses and cows.
They come together.
They have to change.
They have to-- differences from each other.
Then they call the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] They'll come-- I was there.
He was [INAUDIBLE] he was-- how could you make-- you know-- you know the difference between this.
I'll just give you example.
Then, comes [NON-ENGLISH].
You have to buy every-- buy every time-- every-- you have to buy something.
They call up the farmer, [NON-ENGLISH].
He has the-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] He says, because, he says, I only have fertilizer.
[NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
That's all I could give.
He says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] That sounds funny.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] you know the way it tastes.
But that time, that's was the life.
Then, I'll come to the point which I'll bring you out how I come there to Lokacze.
Then, suddenly, they find out I have hided leather and I make leather with a-- a little-- that machine with the hands, you know.
Find it out.
Who told them?
You know the communists-- they told them.
My mother says, you better hide yourself.
Because they was looking-- three police was in this house.
And they're looking all over.
In middle of the night, I run away.
I was already hided in the roof.
And I run away to my aunt in Lokacze, in the other city.
And I was hided there.
A month, two, three.
They bring me food into the hole.
And I don't see the light all together.
And suddenly, she says, listen.
The-- one guy, he says, I cannot hold him.
But you, I have to.
Why?
I want to get married.
You get married with my-- with my daughter, which I know already, from years and years, since childhood.
She was in 1921, I think.
You have to-- I could get-- I married her, get free, and then-- no.
Right away, I'll tell you what.
I told the rabbi, which it was in this country.
And he come in middle of the night, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
He came over, the rabbi Volcan.
He die in America, over here.
Volcan.
He [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] and we went away to Luck-- L-U-C-K.
My wife-- Michla.
M-I-C-H-E-L.
Michla.
Kessel.
Because the children are some-- your cousins are called Kessel.
K-A-- K-E-- Kessel. double S-E-L.
And-- and she ask, I got $500 American money.
I'll give it to you.
Says, OK.
And then she gave me food.
And the cousin I know was the-- she-- I'm older than her.
And I no remember when she was a child even.
And I got married.
Yeah.
